ideas.title: Lazy polars for discovery feature matricesвЂ”defer .to_numpy() until first MI compute
ideas.why: Discovery currently materializes full (n, F) float32 feature matrices into RAM even when F>1000; on a 100GB polars frame this doubles memory. Polars lazy() + collect() at the MI-compute leaf could keep columns native until binning.
ideas.how: Wrap the discovery feature-matrix builder in polars lazy(). Defer column extract via _extract_column_array until _prebin_feature_columns and _mi_pair_bin actually read them. Touch: discovery/_fit.py (feature matrix build), discovery/screening.py (_prebin_feature_columns to accept lazy Series iterator).
ideas.test_sketch: Bench MI discovery (n=100k, F=100, 5 bases) on both eager _to_numpy and lazy polars paths; assert bit-identical MI gains, measure peak memory (lazy should be lower), and measure wall time (lazy may add column-by-column overhead).

ideas.title: Vectorise per-transform param fit across CV foldsвЂ”batch _linear_residual_fit on fold stacks
ideas.why: Per-fold transform re-fitting (honest per-fold params) loops over N_folds sequential lstsq calls in _eval_stats.refit_transform_on_fold; vectorizing the N_folds x (2,2) linear systems into a single batched solve could amortise numpy dispatch overhead.
ideas.how: In _eval.py, when evaluating a _linear_residual* transform across K CV folds, pre-stack all K fold-level (y, base) pairs into a (K*2, 2) QR-decomposed normal-equations matrix, then backsolve all K alphas/betas in one call. Requires changes to refit_transform_on_fold to accept a fold-list input and return K fitted params.
ideas.test_sketch: Profile _tiny_cv_rmse_y_scale with _linear_residual on 5-fold CV; measure per-fold lstsq time before/after batching. Bench at n_fold=5, n_train=10k to capture the dispatch overhead. Assert params are identical to the sequential path.

ideas.title: Cache prebinned feature matrix across mi_sample iterationsвЂ”reuse same bins for all bases
ideas.why: Discovery pre-bins the feature matrix once (_prebin_feature_columns), but on a second discovery run with different config the binning is recomputed. The binned codes are deterministic on the sampled rows; caching them keyed by (data_signature, nbins, sample_idx) avoids O(n*F*log(n)) quantile cost on re-discovery.
ideas.how: Extend DiscoveryCache (cache.py) to store and retrieve prebinned arrays keyed by content hash + nbins. Add a _prebinned_cache_key function. Wire into fit() before the base loop: check cache, use cached binned if hit, populate on first compute. Serialize the int16/int32 binned matrix via numpy save/load.
ideas.test_sketch: Run discovery twice on the same data with different mi_estimator but same nbins. Measure wall time on the second run (should skip ~500ms of quantile work). Assert MI gains are identical (bit-identical prebinning).

ideas.title: Vectorise MI aggregation inside the per-base loopвЂ”compute MI(T, X_{all}) once per transform, not per base
ideas.why: For unary transforms that ignore base, discovery currently re-evaluates MI(T, X_full) per base candidate, even though unary T is independent of base. Three extra MI aggregations (one per dummy base) = 3x cost for an unmodified result.
ideas.how: Lift the unary per-feature MI vector (_mi_per_feature_y_fixed or per-feature aggregation) out of the base loop into build_unary_base_context. Reuse it for every unary spec's aggregation. Compute _mi_kwargs once. Touch: discovery/_eval.py (build_unary_base_context), discovery/_fit.py (threading model to use the cached context).
ideas.test_sketch: Profile discovery on a config with unary transforms enabled. Measure MI-compute time in _eval_one_transform for unary specs before/after. Assert n_unary MI calls drops from 3*n_unary to 1*n_unary. Bench on n=50k, F=100, n_bases=3, with 2 unary specs.

ideas.title: Numba-JIT the per-base correlation deduplication loop (near_collinear_keep_mask)
ideas.why: near_collinear_keep_mask walks O(B^2) pairwise correlations on the per-base feature subset; on 50+ bases this is a hot path (100 ms+ on typical data). The loop is pure numpyвЂ”Pearson correlation & boolean indexingвЂ”ideal for numba.njit.
ideas.how: Carve _compute_pairwise_corr_dedup into a pure-numba kernel that returns the keep mask. Gate it on n_cols (skip JIT for tiny subsets <10 cols) and n_rows > some threshold. Touch: discovery/_eval_stats.py, and add _benchmarks/bench_collinear_mask.py to measure the win.
ideas.test_sketch: Bench near_collinear_keep_mask on a (10k, 50) random matrix before/after numba. Measure wall time at nbins=12. Expected 3-5x speedup (Python loop overhead). Assert bit-identical output.

ideas.title: Dispatch _safe_abs_corr_all to numba / cupy for large feature matrices in forbidden-base filtering
ideas.why: The correlation gate (_safe_abs_corr_all) runs on every (base, candidate) pair during the leakage filter; on 100 base candidates + 200 feature columns this is 100s Г— vectorised 200-column correlation. Currently pure numpy einsum; at n=50k,F=200 it is 50-100ms per gate, called 3-5x per discovery.
ideas.how: Split _safe_abs_corr_all into: numpy (reference), njit (single-thread), and optional cupy (for GPU). Dispatch on data size (n > 20k, F > 100) to numba. Keep the einsum for small cases. Touch: discovery/screening.py, add bench to _benchmarks/bench_safe_corr_dispatch.py.
ideas.test_sketch: Bench _safe_abs_corr_all(y: 50k, X: 50kГ—200) on numpy vs numba before/after. Expected 2-4x win. Gate dispatch at n=20k; verify unrolled correctness on 5 seeds.

ideas.title: Memoize per-bin RMSE lookups in tiny-model CVвЂ”cache binв†’RMSE map across folds
ideas.why: Tiny-model CV computes per-bin RMSE (_per_bin_rmse) to screen bases; when the same base is tested across multiple inner folds, each fold re-computes residuals & binning. The per-bin map is deterministic on y_scale; memoizing it (keyed by bin edges, y_scale_median) avoids redundant reduction.
ideas.how: Thread a thread-safe dict cache into _tiny_cv_rmse_y_scale; key: (frozenset of edges, y_scale_median). Reuse cached RMSE per bin on fold 2..K. Touch: discovery/_screening_tiny.py, add a dict-based memo with lock.
ideas.test_sketch: Profile _tiny_cv_rmse_y_scale_multiseed on a single base over 5-fold CV. Measure per_bin_rmse call count before/after (should drop from 5 to 1 after warm). Bench on n_train=10k, n_bins=12, 5 folds, 2 seeds.

ideas.title: Cache kernel_tuning_cache dispatch thresholds for MI & correlation kernels
ideas.why: Discovery calls numba MI & correlation kernels millions of times but dispatch overhead (env-var check, threshold lookup) is ~4us per call. On 50k columns the overhead alone is seconds. Hoisting dispatch out of hot loops requires knowing the right backend threshold per hardware; kernel_tuning_cache already solves this for polynomial eval but isn't wired to MI.
ideas.how: Add _mi_dispatch and _correlation_dispatch entries to kernel_tuning_cache following the pattern in pyutilz. Measure n=(10k, 100k, 1M) crossovers for both MI binning and safe_abs_corr on a reference bench. Wire into discovery's outer base loop to hoist the dispatch decision.
ideas.test_sketch: Bench MI & correlation on n в€€ {10k, 100k, 1M} and measure numba vs numpy crossover. Build a kernel_tuning_cache entry. Verify dispatch fires the right backend 10M times (measure amortised per-call cost; should be <1us vs 4us current).

ideas.title: Vectorise _extract_column_array to gather multiple columns in one pass (batch gather)
ideas.why: Discovery samples rows and extracts F columns one-by-one via _extract_column_array; polars gather is fast but 500 sequential gather calls (one per column) still add up. Vectorising via polars select([cols]) + gather(rows) in one pass could batch the gather kernel.
ideas.how: Add _extract_columns_batch(df, cols: List[str], rows) that gathers all cols together. In screening feature-matrix construction, replace the column loop with a single batch gather call. Touch: discovery/screening.py (_extract_column_array wrapper or new function), discovery/_fit.py (feature matrix builder).
ideas.test_sketch: Bench _extract_columns_batch on (100k, 500) polars frame, gather 10k rows, 500 cols individually vs batch. Expected 2-3x on gather kernel amortization (depends on polars internals). Measure on both polars and pandas paths.

ideas.title: Early-stop MI aggregation when remaining bases cannot overtake top-K
ideas.why: Discovery sorts bases by MI gain and keeps top-K; once the top-K is locked, any remaining base whose upper bound on MI gain (e.g. mi_y alone, before subtracting mi_gain threshold) cannot reach the K-th score can be skipped. This saves ~10-20% of MI evals on large candidate sets.
ideas.how: After each base's MI compute, check if the remaining candidates' potential (max possible gain on that base) can reach the top-K threshold. If not, mark for early-stop. Touch: discovery/_fit.py (per-base loop logic), discovery/_eval.py (return upper-bound estimate).
ideas.test_sketch: Bench discovery with 100 base candidates, top_k=10. Measure MI evals before/after early-stop. Expected 15-25% reduction (depends on distribution of gains). Assert top-10 final results are identical (early-stopped bases below top-10).

ideas.title: Lazy polars for estimator.predict()вЂ”batch gather base/group columns only once
ideas.why: CompositeTargetEstimator._predict_unclipped extracts base_column and (optionally) group_column per predict call via separate _extract_base / _extract_groups calls on large frames. Two separate polars gather calls instead of one batch gather wastes kernel dispatch.
ideas.how: Add _extract_base_and_groups_batch() that gathers both in one polars call. Reuse across _predict_unclipped paths. Small cleanup but consistent with the discovery batching philosophy. Touch: estimator/_predict.py.
ideas.test_sketch: Bench predict on a 100k-row frame with base_column + group_column. Measure per-predict time before/after batching. Expected 5-10% on polars (two gather calls vs one). Pandas sees no win (iloc is already single-pass).

ideas.title: Add Tweedie power transform (T = sign(y)*|y|^p) with shape-parameter MLE
ideas.why: Tweedie generalizes log (pв†’1) and square-root (p=0.5) for count/power-law data; current Yeo-Johnson works only when skew/kurtosis is the dominant issue, not when the base generative model is a Tweedie mean-variance relationship. Missing a major accuracy win on count targets (insurance claims, demand forecasting).
ideas.how: Add new unary transform in transforms/unary.py with MLE-fitted shape parameter (scipy.optimize.brent on Tweedie.logpdf), forward/inverse/domain wired into registry. Test on Poisson-like synthetic + biz_value test on count-heavy fixture.
ideas.test_sketch: Generate y ~ Tweedie(mu=10, p=1.5, phi=2); fit log_y vs tweedie_y; measure MI(T, X) and OOF RMSE on held-out split. Tweedie should dominate log by >=0.15 MI gain.

ideas.title: Implement Theil-Sen slope estimator for linear_residual as robust alternative to trimmed-LS
ideas.why: Theil-Sen (nonparametric median-of-slopes) is more outlier-resistant than OLS + trimming; current linear_residual_robust uses trimmed-LS which can still misfit if outliers are sparse but extreme. Theil-Sen has proven 96th-percentile breakdown point vs 80% for 3*MAD trimming.
ideas.how: Add _linear_residual_theil_sen_fit in transforms/linear.py, compute all pairwise slopes, take median. Return {alpha: median_slope, beta: median(y - alpha*base)}. O(nВІ) at fit scales down via subsampling (default 10k rows); wire into registry as new transform. Compare wall vs bench expectation (trade accuracy for speed, gate on n_train).
ideas.test_sketch: Generate y = 10*base + 5 + outliers(5% Cauchy). Fit linear_residual_robust vs linear_residual_theil_sen; measure alpha/beta error and OOF RMSE. Theil-Sen alpha error <0.5%, robust LS >2% on this combo.

ideas.title: Signed-log transform: T = sign(y)*log(|y| + offset) for mixed-sign targets
ideas.why: Log/Yeo-Johnson work on positive or mixed targets but neither cleanly models the structure of targets with both large positive and large negative values (e.g., returns, profit/loss, PnL). Signed-log preserves relative scale in both tails without clipping.
ideas.how: Add new unary _signed_log_fit / forward / inverse / domain in transforms/unary.py. Fit offset so min(|y|) + offset > 1 (avoid log(~0)). Test on synthetic heavy-tail mixed-sign target; wire into registry.
ideas.test_sketch: y ~ t(df=3) centered at 0 (heavy-tail mixed-sign). Fit signed_log_y vs yeo_johnson_y; measure MI(T, X) on random feature matrix. signed_log should win by >=0.10 MI on excess-kurtosis targets.

ideas.title: Target-encoding residual: T = y - encode(categorical_base), learns per-category means with Ridge shrinkage
ideas.why: High-cardinality categorical bases (region, store_id, user_segment with 100-1k levels) are currently ignored by discovery (MI screening handles them poorly if they're highly grouped). A target-encoded residual learns the per-category mean with Bayesian shrinkage, modeling y variation within the main category structure.
ideas.how: Add _target_encoded_residual_fit / forward / inverse in transforms/linear.py. At fit: group y by base (categorical), compute per-group mean, apply Ridge shrinkage (lambda=1.0) toward global mean. At predict: lookup group mean or fall back to global. Forward: T = y - group_mean. Inverse: y_hat = T_hat + group_mean. Wire into registry with requires_groups or requires_categorical flag.
ideas.test_sketch: Generate y = 5*group_id + 2*base_cont + noise; categorical base with 50 groups. Fit linear_residual + target_encoded_residual; measure MI gain and RMSE. Target-encoded should dominate when categorical explains >30% of y variance.

ideas.title: Region-adaptive transform selection: partition feature space (base quantiles), select best transform per region
ideas.why: Single global transform often sub-optimal; e.g., y=base at low base (use linear_residual) but yв‰€sqrt(base) at high base (use ratio). Current system fits one transform globally, leaving high bias in regions where the relationship changes structure.
ideas.how: Add discovery hook _select_adaptive_transforms_per_region in discovery/__init__.py. After MI screening top-K candidates: for each (base, transform), partition train by base quantiles (5-10 bins), compute T per bin, measure MI(T | bin) per bin. Keep (base, transform, bin) tuples where bin-conditional MI significantly exceeds global. Emit multi-spec 'adaptive_<name>' with per-bin fitted params.
ideas.test_sketch: Generate y piecewise: base<5 в†’ linear, base>=5 в†’ ratio. Fit discovery with standard selection vs adaptive selection; measure OOF RMSE split by base region. Adaptive wins by >15% RMSE in mismatched regions.

ideas.title: Isotonic regression calibration for ensemble predictions: post-hoc monotone calibration on OOF residuals
ideas.why: CompositeTargetEstimator produces unbiased point estimates but ensemble predictions can be miscalibrated (e.g., avg residual != 0 on certain prediction ranges). Isotonic regression is model-agnostic, enforces monotonicity (confidence в†‘ в†’ accuracy в†‘), and is proven optimal on finite samples.
ideas.how: Add calibrate_isotonic(X_cal, y_cal) method to CompositeTargetEstimator / CompositeCrossTargetEnsemble. Fit sklearn.isotonic.IsotonicRegression on (y_pred_cal, y_true_cal - y_pred_cal), store fitted regressor. At predict: adjust predictions via isotonic(raw_pred). Compare to temperature scaling and conformal on validation set.
ideas.test_sketch: Fit composite ensemble on train/val split; measure OOF calibration error (MAE of mean residual by prediction decile). Add isotonic calibration; measure post-calibration error. Isotonic should reduce error by >=20% vs raw.

ideas.title: Mondrian/group-conditional conformal prediction: separate conformal radii per (base_column, value) group
ideas.why: Current conformal_quantile uses a single global radius, which over-covers groups with low noise and under-covers heteroscedastic groups. Mondrian conformal computes per-group quantiles, yielding tighter bands where data is clean and wider bands where it's noisy (heteroscedasticity-adaptive).
ideas.how: Add calibrate_conformal_mondrian(X_cal, y_cal, group_column, alpha) in conformal.py. Partition cal by group_column value, compute residual quantile per group separately. Store dict {group_value: radius}. At predict_interval_mondrian: extract group value from X, lookup group-specific radius. Graceful fallback to global radius for unseen groups.
ideas.test_sketch: Generate y with heteroscedastic noise: small noise in base<0, large noise in base>0. Fit conformal vs conformal_mondrian on base groups; measure coverage + avg interval width per group. Mondrian should maintain >95% coverage while reducing avg width in low-noise group.

ideas.title: Normalized conformal (CQR with heteroscedasticity weighting): use residual/sigma_hat(x) as nonconformity score
ideas.why: Conformal currently uses absolute residuals (homoscedastic score), giving equal-width bands regardless of local noise. Normalized/CQR scores use residual/estimated_sigma, producing adaptive-width intervals that are tighter where the inner model is more confident.
ideas.how: Extend conformal.py: after predict(X_cal), estimate per-row residual variance via local regression (e.g., |residual| ~ base via isotonic/robust regression). Compute normalized scores = residuals / sigma_hat. Use _signed_finite_sample_quantile on normalized scores in calibrate_conformal_cqr. At predict: y_hat +/- q * sigma_hat(X_test) gives variable-width intervals.
ideas.test_sketch: Heteroscedastic target: noise ~ sqrt(base). Fit standard conformal vs normalized; measure interval width in [low-base, high-base] regions. Normalized should be significantly narrower in low-noise region while keeping overall coverage >=1-alpha.

ideas.title: Multi-criteria transform ranking: Pareto frontier of (MI gain, domain coverage %, transform invertibility score)
ideas.why: Current discovery ranks transforms by MI gain alone; transforms with high MI but <50% domain coverage or poor numerical stability are promoted. A Pareto selection keeps candidates on the accuracy/robustness frontier, filtering out high-MI-but-risky selections.
ideas.how: Extend _fit.py: after MI screening, compute per-spec invertibility (rank-condition number for linear, oscillation check for monotonic, eps-floor utilization for ratio/logratio). Compute domain coverage % (valid_mask.mean()). Build 3-D score (mi_gain, domain_frac, invertibility_score). Filter to Pareto frontier (keep specs not dominated on all 3 axes). Optionally gate discovery on minimum domain_frac (e.g., >70%).
ideas.test_sketch: Synthetic with 5 base candidates; some high-MI but <30% valid domain (e.g., log_y on mixed-sign y). Run standard discovery vs Pareto-filtered; measure OOF RMSE + % of specs with domain_frac<70% in final top-K. Pareto should filter out low-coverage specs.

ideas.title: WAIC/LOO-CV validation of transform choice: hold-out-one-fold information criterion for discovery selection
ideas.why: MI gain is a ranking proxy but not a generalization predictor. WAIC (Widely Applicable Information Criterion) and LOO-CV select transforms that genuinely reduce expected prediction error, not just MI. Especially valuable when multiple transforms tie on MI.
ideas.how: Add _compute_waic_per_spec and _compute_looCV_per_spec in discovery/_eval.py. For each spec, compute T on train, fit inner (or use pre-fitted tiny model from rerank stage), score OOF log-loss / RMSE, estimate WAIC via Pareto-smoothed importance sampling. Sort by WAIC (lower=better) and rerank the MI-filtered top-K by WAIC; keep the WAIC winner + next few tied candidates.
ideas.test_sketch: Generate target with N(0,1) noise. Two transforms tie on MI gain (e.g., linear_residual_robust and linear_residual_theil_sen at similar MI). Run discovery with MI-only ranking vs WAIC rerank; measure which is in final top-3. WAIC should promote the one with lower OOF loss.

ideas.title: Better alpha/beta estimation: empirical Bayes shrinkage with data-driven priors for linear_residual coefficients
ideas.why: Current Bayesian alpha uses a flat prior (maximum entropy but weak informativity). On small n_train or weak correlation structures, the posterior is wide. Empirical Bayes learns a prior from data (e.g., prior mean from a quick KNN correlation estimate), giving tighter posteriors and lower MSE on new bases.
ideas.how: Extend bayesian_alpha_fit in discovery/bayesian.py: before fitting the conjugate posterior, estimate a weak prior on alpha via nearest-neighbor correlation (top-5 features' correlations with y). Use the empirical-Bayes update: mix the weak KNN-derived prior with the flat prior using a credibility weight. Return posterior with prior strength in the result dict.
ideas.test_sketch: Generate y = alpha*base + noise on n_train=30 (small sample). Fit with flat prior vs empirical-Bayes prior; measure alpha-MSE on 1000 test bases. Empirical Bayes should reduce MSE by >=15% on small n when prior is informative.

ideas.title: Interaction-aware transform pairing: auto-detect when multiple transforms should be jointly selected (e.g., separate residuals for two dominant bases)
ideas.why: Discovery ranks (base, transform) pairs independently. When two bases capture orthogonal structure (e.g., y = base1 + base2 + noise), selecting just one leaves the other's signal in the residual. Current multi-base selection only applies linear_residual; other transforms aren't jointly explored.
ideas.how: Add _detect_interaction_bases in discovery/_auto_base.py. After MI top-K, measure residual MI between top-1 and top-2 base candidates. If high (>0.7*top-1 MI), try stacking transforms: fit top-2 with linear_residual_multi, then fit a SECOND transform on the residual. Emit a chained spec 'stacked_<name1>_<name2>'. Compare chain MI vs single top-K; keep if superior.
ideas.test_sketch: y = base1 + 0.8*base2 + noise. Discovery finds both via MI. Check if stacking linear_residual(base1) + linear_residual(residual, base2) achieves higher combined MI than single top-1 linear_residual(base1). Should detect complementary structure.

ideas.title: Multi-output composite target support (vector y)
ideas.why: Current CompositeTargetEstimator assumes scalar y; production datasets often have multiple correlated targets (e.g., demand forecasting + inventory + revenue simultaneously). Users must loop over targets manually, losing correlation structure and multi-target discovery synergies.
ideas.how: Extend CompositeTargetEstimator to accept (n, k) y at fit/predict. Each target gets its own per-column base_column + transform_name, discovered jointly via multi-output MI (mutual information between X and each y column, with target correlation penalty). Store fitted params as {target_0: {...}, target_1: {...}} dict. Transform registry unchanged (apply per-column). Modify discovery to use scipy.stats.kendalltau / partial_correlation to capture target dependencies during base ranking. Files: estimator/_estimator.py (fit/predict logic), discovery/_fit.py (multi-target screening), transforms/multi_output.py (new), tests/test_composite_multi_output.py.
ideas.test_sketch: Synthetic (n=1000, y shape (n,3) with y[:,1] = 0.9*y[:,0] + noise, y[:,2] = -y[:,0] + noise). Composite on y[:,0] + y[:,1] should pick same base+transform; discovery correlation gate should suppress redundant y[:,2] base unless gain exceeds threshold. Wall time <= 1.2Г— single-output for same feature count.

ideas.title: GLM-family composite targets (Poisson/Tweedie/Gamma log-link)
ideas.why: CompositeTargetEstimator is regression-only (L2 loss в†’ y-scale targets). Count/rate/duration domains need generalized-linear composites: Poisson (log y), Tweedie (power link), Gamma (log y), survival (log-duration). Wrapping a Poisson GLM inner without a composite pre-transform wastes the structureвЂ”users resort to log(y+1) hacks that break the calibration contract.
ideas.how: Add three new unary-transform-style classes to transforms/glm_family.py: PoissonLogComposite (forward: log(y+eps), inverse: exp(T), domain: y>0), TweedieComposite (power-link with exponent param), GammaComposite (log-link + shape-fitting). Each exposes fit(y, base=None, params) returning {fitted_scale, fitted_shape} dicts. CompositeTargetEstimator detects GLM via a new `distribution` kwarg (default='normal' for L2, new 'poisson'/'tweedie'/'gamma' options). Discovery skips these for now (use unary only); future: conditional MI for GLM domains. Files: transforms/glm_family.py (new), estimator/_estimator.py (distribution dispatch), conformal.py (Poisson pinball alternative).
ideas.test_sketch: Synthetic Poisson (y = Pois(exp(X[:,0] + 0.1*X[:,1]))); fit CompositeTargetEstimator with distribution='poisson', expect prediction calibration on holdout (mean(y_test) в‰€ mean(y_hat_test), not y_hat clipped to wrong bounds). MAE on Poisson в‰¤ 1.3Г— baseline log(y+1) transform.

ideas.title: Quantile-loss composite targets (pinball / asymmetric)
ideas.why: CompositeTargetEstimator + conformal.conformal_quantile produce *prediction intervals* via split-conformal. But a quantile-loss inner (LightGBM `objective='quantile'`) learns conditional quantiles natively. Compositing a quantile target T_q = f_q(y, base) lets the inner focus on residual quantiles instead of quantile bias, improving conditional calibration. Currently users cannot specify which quantile to composite or batch multiple quantiles together.
ideas.how: Add QuantileComposite transform (nonlinear.py): forward: y_q = quantile(y, q) - alpha*quantile(base, q) fitted via weighted pinball, inverse: y = invquantile(T_q + alpha*quantile(base, q), q). Expose `quantile_level` param in Transform (default 0.5 for median). Extend discovery to handle quantile bases: _screening_tiny.py detects if inner has predict_quantile, ranks bases by MI(y_q, X | base) for each q. Store fitted_params['quantile_level']. Files: transforms/extended.py (new QuantileComposite section), estimator/_estimator.py (quantile dispatch), discovery/_screening_tiny.py (quantile detection).
ideas.test_sketch: Synthetic (n=1000, heteroscedastic y with std increasing in X[:,0]). Composite quantile (0.1, 0.5, 0.9) on y; inner LightGBM quantile. Coverage of the composite 0.9-interval on holdout >= 88% (conformal floor), pinball loss < 1.15Г— unconditional quantile.

ideas.title: Group-conditional composite discovery (stratified bases)
ideas.why: CompositeTargetDiscovery ranks bases globally via MI(y, base). In hierarchical/grouped data (e.g., per-user, per-region), the best base often *varies by group*. Currently the only option is run_composite_discovery(...) once per group, then manually stitch resultsвЂ”no principled way to detect when group heterogeneity is large enough to justify the overhead or how to blend per-group + global bases.
ideas.how: Add group_column kwarg to CompositeTargetDiscovery.fit(). After base ranking, compute per-group MI(y_g, base_g) matrix; gate on a heterogeneity index (e.g. max_within_group_corr - mean_global_corr > threshold). If heterogeneous, run _auto_base per-group in parallel (joblib), collect top-k per group, then refit discovery on the union {global_base, group_1_base, ...}. Store group_mapping_ dict {group_value: (base_column, transform_name)}. CompositeTargetEstimator.predict dispatches on group column if mapping available. Files: discovery/__init__.py (group_column kwarg), discovery/_auto_base.py (group loop), estimator/_predict.py (dispatch logic).
ideas.test_sketch: Synthetic (n=2000, 4 groups with group-specific base structure: group A benefits from lag, group B from rolling mean). Discovery with group_column should detect heterogeneity, assign different bases per group. Holdout RMSE improvement >= 5% vs global-base composite.

ideas.title: Automated feature-interaction discovery for bases
ideas.why: Current base ranking in discovery/_auto_base.py ranks individual features by MI. A 2-way interaction (e.g. feature_a * feature_b as a synthetic base) often outperforms its component features individually. No systematic way to discover interaction bases without exponential search; users must manually engineer promising interactions.
ideas.how: Add interaction_max_order param (default 2) to CompositeTargetDiscovery. After individual-feature base ranking, generate candidate interaction bases via itertools.combinations up to interaction_max_order. Score each via MI(y, interaction_base) / complexity_penalty (the penalty suppresses degenerate high-cardinality interactions). Evaluate only top-N individuals' combinations to stay tractable. Use generate_interaction_bases() pattern (already in transforms/interaction_bases.py). Store interaction formulas in CompositeProvenance. Files: discovery/_auto_base.py (interaction loop), discovery/screening.py (MI scoring for interactions), provenance.py (formula tracking).
ideas.test_sketch: Synthetic (y = 0.8*X[:,0]*X[:,1] + 0.2*noise, interaction dominates univariate). Discovery with interaction_max_order=2 should rank X[:,0]*X[:,1] in top-3 bases. Wall time increase <= 2.5Г— compared to univariate-only discovery on same feature count.

ideas.title: sklearn Pipeline / ColumnTransformer native integration
ideas.why: CompositeTargetEstimator is an sklearn BaseEstimator, but nested inside a Pipeline as the final regressor step, the pre_pipeline (feature transform) is not automatically wired into the inner estimator. Users must manually call pre_pipeline.transform(X) before passing to CompositeTargetEstimator.predict, defeating the Pipeline contract. sklearn Pipelines with ColumnTransformer (e.g., scaling numerics, encoding categoricals) can then silently bypass the composite setup.
ideas.how: Extend CompositeTargetEstimator.fit to detect and capture a parent Pipeline's ColumnTransformer (via sklearn's metadata routing / _fit_transform_one introspection if available, else attribute walk). Store as fitted_pre_pipeline_. At predict time, auto-apply if detected. Alternatively, add a pre_pipeline param to CompositeTargetEstimator.__init__ (explicit, not auto-detected), mirroring the existing PrePipelinePredictShim but surfaced directly. Update estimator/__init__.py + _estimator.py (fit/predict dispatch). Files: estimator/_estimator.py (pre_pipeline kwarg + dispatch), post_shim.py (unify with existing shim if needed), tests/test_composite_pipeline_integration.py.
ideas.test_sketch: sklearn Pipeline with ColumnTransformer (scale numerics) в†’ CompositeTargetEstimator (linear_residual on lag). Fit via pipeline.fit(X_raw, y). Predict on unseen X_raw should auto-transform via ColumnTransformer before applying composite inverse. Predictions bit-identical to manual pre-transform + CompositeTargetEstimator.predict.

ideas.title: partial_fit / incremental learning support
ideas.why: CompositeTargetEstimator is fit-only; large datasets or streaming scenarios need incremental updates. The inner estimator (e.g. SGDRegressor) may support partial_fit, but the composite transform coefficients (alpha, beta, MAD caps) are fixed after initial fit. No way to update transform params as new data arrives without a full refit.
ideas.how: Add partial_fit(X, y, base, sample_weight=None, **kwargs) method to CompositeTargetEstimator. Pass through to inner.partial_fit if available. For transform coefficients (alpha/beta in linear_residual), implement optional incremental averaging: keep a running OLS mean on the fitted base/residual pairs and update alpha/beta via Welford's algorithm. This preserves the fixed-transform contract (T is deterministic given y, base) while staying calibrated to drift. MAD caps + y-clip bounds stay from original fit (no refit). Store iteration count for diagnostics. Files: estimator/_estimator.py (partial_fit method), estimator/_update.py (already exists for online_refit, extend it), tests/test_composite_partial_fit.py.
ideas.test_sketch: Synthetic stream (1000 total rows, split into 10 batches of 100). Fit on batch 0, then partial_fit on batches 1-9. Final alpha estimate should converge toward the batch-wise joint OLS on all 1000 rows, within 5% (Welford drift from true OLS is small at this scale).

ideas.title: Monotonic-constraint passthrough to inner estimator
ideas.why: CompositeTargetEstimator wraps a base estimator and learns T = f(y, base). Many inner estimators (LightGBM, XGBoost, sklearn isotonic) support monotonic constraints on features. Currently there is no way to specify that the composite transform should enforce monotonic behavior or pass constraints to the innerвЂ”users must manually manage this outside the wrapper.
ideas.how: Add monotonic_constraints param to CompositeTargetEstimator.__init__: a dict {feature_name: ('increasing' | 'decreasing' | None)} or passthrough to inner's constraints. At fit time, validate that the selected transform (e.g. logratio, quantile_residual) is monotone-preserving (i.e., monotone in y implies monotone in T); non-monotone transforms (e.g. centered_ratio, pairwise_interaction) raise a clear error. Pass constraints through to inner.fit(**inner_fit_kwargs including constraints). For monotone-transform chains (composition of monotone transforms preserves monotonicity), update transform logic to document this property. Files: estimator/_estimator.py (constraints param, domain check), transforms/__init__.py (add monotonicity metadata to Transform), discovery/_filter.py (exclude non-monotone transforms if constraints are set).
ideas.test_sketch: Synthetic monotone (y monotone in X[:,0], other features noise). Composite with monotonic_constraints={'feature_0': 'increasing'} using LightGBM inner. Model predictions should be monotone-increasing in X[:,0] on holdout (check via dydx sign). Training on a non-monotone inner without constraints should raise ValueError.

ideas.title: Count / survival / duration composite targets (specialized likelihoods)
ideas.why: Beyond Poisson (integer counts), survival analysis (censored durations) and duration models (positive-valued times) need specialized composites. A survival composite should decompose the hazard/baseline into base + residual, respecting censoring. No support exists; users must work around via Kaplan-Meier + log-transform hacks.
ideas.how: Add transforms/survival.py with SurvivalComposite (forward: log-duration, inverse: exp(T), fit: censoring-aware via median[uncensored], domain: y>0 + censor_mask input), DurationComposite (similar but ignores censoring). Extend CompositeTargetEstimator to accept optional censor_mask at fit/predict; pass to transform fit/inverse. Update discovery to skip survival composites if censor_mask is None (safety guard). For CoxPH-style inner estimators, implement a shim that wraps partial_hazard scores. Files: transforms/survival.py (new), estimator/_estimator.py (censor_mask param), estimator/_predict.py (censoring dispatch), discovery/_filter.py (safety gate).
ideas.test_sketch: Synthetic survival (Weibull duration, 30% censoring). Composite with censor_mask, LightGBM inner on log-duration. Holdout Harrell's C-index >= 0.75 vs naive log(y) regressor, calibration plot shows good alignment on uncensored tail.

ideas.title: Rich HTML repr for CompositeTargetEstimator in Jupyter
ideas.why: Sklearn estimators show _repr_html_ in notebooks; CompositeTargetEstimator has no visual summary. Users can't quickly inspect what transform/base/params are active without calling .get_params() or reading fitted_params_ manually. Common in Jupyter workflows.
ideas.how: Add _repr_html_() method to CompositeTargetEstimator (estimator/_estimator.py) that renders a Jupyter-friendly table: transform name + formula (from provenance), base_column, fitted_params (alpha/beta/MAD), domain_violation_rate, y_clip_hit_rate. Use existing _format_transform_formulas helpers. Zero copy / pure HTML generation.
ideas.test_sketch: test_composite_estimator_repr_html_renders_without_error + test_repr_html_contains_transform_name_and_formula + test_repr_html_shows_fitted_params_and_stats

ideas.title: Auto-detect base candidates from data shape/correlation
ideas.why: CompositeTargetDiscovery requires explicit base_candidates list. On wide (1000+ columns) datasets, users don't know which columns are good base candidates; they either guess (biased toward first columns) or use the full feature set (slow MI screening on junk columns). No guidance in docstring.
ideas.how: Add optional auto_detect_bases mode to CompositeTargetDiscoveryConfig + discovery._auto_base module. Heuristics: (1) correlation with y > threshold (e.g., 0.3) -- strong univariate link; (2) low cardinality + high frequency (e.g., category counts / time bins) -- good for residual; (3) user can pass ranks/scores. Screens to top-K by a score combo. Wire into fit() early, before MI computation. Benchmark on 10+ real datasets (time to evaluate top-K vs full width).
ideas.test_sketch: test_auto_detect_bases_correlation_ranks_lag_first + test_auto_detect_bases_time_bins_ranked_high + test_auto_detect_reduces_mi_screening_calls + test_auto_detect_bases_config_override_disables_it

ideas.title: Diagnostic plot: conformal interval width vs MI gain tradeoff
ideas.why: Users fitted conformal intervals (via calibrate_conformal) but have no way to visualize the data-cost: whether the interval is tight enough to be useful or so wide it's uninformative. Scatter plot of (MI gain for composite, interval width / y_std) shows whether a composite 'pays for itself'.
ideas.how: Add plot_interval_width_vs_gain() to diagnostics.py. Iterates over fitted composites, computes interval width (via conformal_quantile on val residuals), plots scatter colored by transform type. Companion: histogram of interval widths per composite (for spec ranking). Use existing conformal quantile + _lazy_pyplot pattern.
ideas.test_sketch: test_plot_interval_width_vs_gain_returns_figure + test_plot_shows_one_point_per_composite + test_plot_colors_by_transform_name + test_plot_filters_inf_widths

ideas.title: Diagnostic plot: reliability diagram for conformal intervals
ideas.why: Conformal intervals are marginal-valid (coverage ~1-alpha) but users want to see HOW WELL on their data. Reliability diagram (empirical coverage vs alpha, binned by MI gain / transform) reveals whether the calibration is conservative/liberal per data region.
ideas.how: Add plot_conformal_reliability() to diagnostics.py. Splits calibration set into alpha bins (e.g., alpha=0.05/0.10/0.20), computes empirical coverage per bin, plots coverage-vs-alpha with error bands. Overlay theoretical 1-alpha line. Companion histogram of n_cal per alpha. Gate on n_cal >= 100 (too few calibration rows = uninformative plot).
ideas.test_sketch: test_reliability_plot_returns_figure + test_reliability_empirical_coverage_near_1_minus_alpha + test_reliability_gates_on_n_cal + test_reliability_shows_warning_on_small_n

ideas.title: report_to_markdown richer output: discovery metrics matrix + decision tree
ideas.why: Current report_to_markdown() produces a readable paragraph per composite but no MATRIX of decision boundaries: which specs failed the domain filter (why), which passed MI gain but lost to tiny rerank, final ranking. Users must call .report() and .filter_drops() separately to reverse-engineer what happened.
ideas.how: Enhance report_to_markdown() to emit a markdown table: [spec_name | transform | base | mi_y | mi_t | mi_gain | domain_frac | tiny_rmse | final_rank | drop_reason]. Uses existing .report() / .filter_drops(). Append a 'decision tree' summary: 'N candidates passed MI, M passed domain, K passed tiny rerank, L selected'. Markdown-safe table generation (escape | in base column names).
ideas.test_sketch: test_markdown_table_has_all_columns + test_markdown_table_sorted_by_rank + test_markdown_tree_counts_match_specs_report + test_markdown_escapes_pipe_in_names

ideas.title: CompositeTargetEstimator.get_params completeness for sklearn GridSearchCV
ideas.why: CompositeTargetEstimator inherits sklearn BaseEstimator but does NOT implement get_params() override (only __init__ defines the params). sklearn.clone() works (uses __init__ signature) but GridSearchCV with nested params (e.g., base_estimator__n_estimators for a RandomForest inner) fails silently: get_params(deep=True) doesn't recursively expose inner params.
ideas.how: Override get_params(deep=True) in CompositeTargetEstimator to call get_params on base_estimator when deep=True. Mirrors sklearn.Pipeline pattern. Test with RandomForest inner + GridSearchCV on (base_estimator__max_depth, transform_name). Verify clone() + set_params() roundtrip.
ideas.test_sketch: test_get_params_deep_false_returns_own_params_only + test_get_params_deep_true_exposes_base_estimator__params + test_clone_with_nested_params_roundtrips + test_gridsearchcv_tuning_base_estimator_inner_param

ideas.title: One-call convenience wrapper: fit_and_predict_ensemble from discovery
ideas.why: Common pattern: fit discovery, build ensemble from top-K specs, predict. Users must call: CompositeTargetDiscovery().fit() -> export specs -> for each spec build CompositeTargetEstimator -> CompositeCrossTargetEnsemble(). No single-call convenience function. Adds friction, opportunity for config misalignment.
ideas.how: Add fit_predict_ensemble(df, target_col, feature_cols, train_idx, val_idx, base_estimator, ensemble_strategy='weighted_mean', return_provenance=True) to CompositeTargetDiscovery. Orchestrates: fit discovery -> build estimators -> fit ensemble on val_idx -> return ensemble + specs + provenance. Thin wrapper, fully documented with an example in the docstring.
ideas.test_sketch: test_fit_predict_ensemble_returns_fitted_ensemble + test_fit_predict_ensemble_predictions_same_as_manual_build + test_fit_predict_ensemble_with_return_provenance_true

ideas.title: Docstring expansion: CompositeTargetEstimator 'Common Usage' examples
ideas.why: CompositeTargetEstimator.__doc__ has Parameters/Attributes but no usage examples. Users unfamiliar with transform semantics (logratio vs ratio, linear_residual vs diff) have to read the transform .py file to understand the contract. No quick examples of 'when to use which transform'.
ideas.how: Add 'Examples' section to CompositeTargetEstimator docstring. Showcase: (1) simple diff on lag target; (2) logratio on multiplicative noise; (3) linear_residual on linear trend; (4) grouped composite on per-entity data. Use small synthetic data, show fit() -> predict() -> fitted_params_ snapshot. Link to diagnostics.plot_linear_fit() for lag visualization.
ideas.test_sketch: test_docstring_examples_execute_without_error (doctest-style), test_docstring_covers_4_transform_families

ideas.title: Error messages: auto-detect missing base column and suggest forced_keep_columns
ideas.why: When feature selection drops the base column, CompositeTargetEstimator.predict() raises KeyError with a helpful message. But the message assumes the user knows they have a feature-selection step before the wrapper. On a raw DataFrame without explicit FS, the error still triggers if someone inadvertently filters columns before .predict().
ideas.how: Enhance the KeyError message in _extract_base() + _extract_base_matrix() to: (1) show the columns that ARE present (first 15), (2) if calling from predict, suggest checking upstream feature selection / preprocessing. Add a warning to fit() when fitted_params_ is set but the next predict() call is likely to drop the column (detect by checking feature_names_in_ for presence of base_column; warn at fit-time if missing).
ideas.test_sketch: test_keyerror_message_lists_available_columns + test_fit_warns_when_base_missing_from_feature_names_in

ideas.title: Discovery report heuristic: auto-suggest transform family for next trial
ideas.why: When discovery finishes with no-gain (all specs MI(T, X) < MI(y, X)), the user has no guidance on what to try next. Was the base too weak? Should they try other transforms? Should they relax the MI gain threshold? Current fallback is a generic 'no composite found'.
ideas.how: Add suggestion logic at the end of fit() when no specs pass the mi_gain gate. Analyze why they failed: (1) MI gains all near-zero (< 0.001)? Suggest lower eps_mi_gain or try different bases. (2) One transform family dominated? Suggest trying other families. (3) Domain violations high? Suggest dropping invalid rows earlier. Log at INFO level. Wire into report().
ideas.test_sketch: test_discovery_no_gain_suggests_lower_threshold + test_discovery_high_domain_violation_suggests_filter + test_suggestions_appear_in_report

ideas.title: Spec-selection guidance: highlight dominant transforms in discovery report
ideas.why: When discovery returns K=5 top specs, users have no sense of STABILITY: are specs 1-2 far ahead of 3-5 (clear winners), or all bunched (pick any)? Current output is a sorted list with no confidence measures. Borrowing from stability_counts_ (when fit_with_stability_check is used), lack of such info for standard fit().
ideas.how: Add 'dominance' metric to discovery report: ratio of top MI gain to bottom-K MI gain (e.g., 1.5x = 50% better). Compute for top-M specs and for each transform family separately. Render in markdown as a short summary: 'logratio dominates diff (2.3x higher MI gain)'. Use when rendering report_to_markdown(). Optional: compute a ranking-stability heuristic (e.g., how much MI gain changes if you resample 50% of rows) via a quick 3-run perturbation.
ideas.test_sketch: test_dominance_ratio_computed_correctly + test_dominance_in_markdown_report + test_dominance_per_transform_family

ideas.title: NaN-only base guard in domain checks (all transforms)
ideas.why: When base is all-NaN, forward transform returns all-NaN without signaling a domain violation. Downstream estimators then train on empty (NaN) targets, fitting silently produces NaN predictions, and the inversion produces fallback y_train_median everywhere. Today this masquerades as a valid model; it should be caught at fit time.
ideas.how: Add an explicit check in CompositeTargetEstimator.fit after domain_check: if len(y_train_finite) == 0 after masking, raise DomainViolationError with a clear message. Alternatively, update every transform's domain_fit to detect all-NaN base and log a WARNING so discovery filters it out. Files: src/mlframe/training/composite/estimator/_estimator.py (fit path), tests/training/composite/test_composite_edge_all_nan_base.py (new regression test).
ideas.test_sketch: Create X with 100 rows, y with 50 finite + 50 NaN, base column all-NaN. Call fit(X, y, base='base') and expect DomainViolationError. Verify that fit_result is unchanged (no silent empty-target fit).

ideas.title: Constant-column stability for multi-base linear_residual_multi (zero-variance base)
ideas.why: When one of the N bases in linear_residual_multi is constant, the OLS solver (np.linalg.lstsq) becomes rank-deficient and returns NaN alphas. The fallback_predict then masks all predictions to y_train_median, silently losing the valid bases' signal. Today there is no per-base variance check before fitting.
ideas.how: In _linear_residual_multi_fit (src/mlframe/training/composite/transforms/linear.py), add a pre-fit variance check: skip constant bases (var < 1e-24), raise or warn if ALL bases are constant, and return a reduced alpha dict for the non-constant subset. Update _linear_residual_multi_forward/_inverse to handle missing keys (map to 0 alpha). Add a biz_value test: multi-base with one constant base must preserve the non-constant bases' contribution to T_hat.
ideas.test_sketch: Multi-base on (y, [base1_constant, base2_varying]). Fit and verify that base2 contribution (fitted alpha2) is non-zero and drives residual reduction, not masked to fallback. Regression: same setup without the fix should produce NaN alphas.

ideas.title: Single-row / tiny-n (n<5) prediction robustness in conformal_quantile
ideas.why: conformal_quantile uses ceil((n+1)(1-alpha)) to compute a rank. When n=1, rank=ceil(2*0.9)=2 > 1, so it returns inf. This is correct (too few points), but callers downstream may not check for inf and will produce unbounded prediction intervals. Also, the presort-to-numpy in conformal.py may not preserve dtype on ragged / empty residual arrays.
ideas.how: In conformal.py, add a _finalize_interval helper that clips infinite radii to a user-specified fallback (e.g. std(y_train) * c, or the max finite residual if all are inf). Update predict_interval to apply this. Add a biz_value test: conformal with n_cal=2 and alpha=0.1 should produce inf-guarded intervals (not NaN). Files: src/mlframe/training/composite/conformal.py (predict_interval / new _finalize_interval), tests/training/composite/test_biz_val_composite_conformal.py (new).
ideas.test_sketch: Calibrate conformal on n_cal=2 rows with alpha=0.1 (rank > n). Call predict_interval and verify output is finite (not inf) and respects y-train bounds. Test that the fallback width is sensible (e.g., at least std(y_train)).

ideas.title: Polars gather/select vs pandas iloc consistency in domain filters
ideas.why: CompositeTargetDiscovery.fit calls _apply_pre_pipeline_transforms which filters rows by domain_check. The filter path uses pandas .iloc but polars .gather differently, and on a large dataframe with many domain-violating rows, the two can diverge on row ordering or duplicate handling. Zero-copy semantics differ (polars returns a view; pandas returns a copy), so downstream MI computation may see different NaN patterns.
ideas.how: Standardize domain filtering in discovery._filter_features / estimator._estimator.fit: wrap pandas/polars row-selection in a helper that uses polars .filter() uniformly (no dual path). Ensure the mask is computed once and applied identically. Add a regression test: identical X as DataFrame and LazyFrame should produce identical domain-kept row indices. Files: src/mlframe/training/composite/discovery/screening.py (_is_numeric_column neighbor), src/mlframe/training/composite/estimator/_estimator.py.
ideas.test_sketch: Create X as both pandas and polars with the same seed. Fit CompositeTargetEstimator on both, apply domain filter (non-finite base, constant columns). Verify that the surviving indices are identical and predictions on the same test set are identical.

ideas.title: Severe class imbalance (M7) handling in discovery screening MI
ideas.why: When y is highly imbalanced (e.g., 99% y=0, 1% y=1), the MI estimator (bin or KNN) often underestimates signal in the minority class due to binning artifacts or density sparsity. Today there is no stratified or weighted resampling in the screening MI calculation, so imbalanced targets silently get lower MI and the best bases are mis-ranked.
ideas.how: In discovery/screening.py (_mi_per_feature_y_fixed), add an optional stratified-resampling gate: when y.std() < threshold (detect imbalance) or when explicitly passed, resample the binning / KNN pair to equalize class counts before MI calculation. This is detection-only; the actual sampling happens in _mi_pair_bin. Add a config flag CompositeTargetDiscoveryConfig.mi_stratified_imbalance=False (off by default to preserve bit-identity). Add a biz_value test: imbalanced y with a highly-predictive minority-class base should rank higher under stratified MI.
ideas.test_sketch: Create y with 1000 rows (99% class 0, 1% class 1). Base X1 predicts class 1 perfectly, base X2 predicts class 0 perfectly. Under standard MI, X2 may rank higher (entropy dominated by majority). Under stratified MI, X1 should rank >= X2 (equal-weight MI).

ideas.title: Extreme outlier clipping contracts for logratio / ratio (MAD floor degeneracy)
ideas.why: logratio uses MAD(T_train) to soft-cap T_hat before inversion. When T_train is degenerate (all rows produce T near a constant), MAD is near-zero and the soft-cap fails. Today the code has a _MAD_FLOOR_FRAC workaround but it is loose (1e-3 of std(y)) and doesn't adapt to the magnitude of the fitted inverse params (alpha, beta). This can cause silent prediction clipping even on well-behaved training data.
ideas.how: In transforms/nonlinear.py (_logratio_fit / _logratio_inverse), tighten the MAD floor logic: compute it as max(_MAD_FLOOR_FRAC * std(y), 0.1 * MAD(T_train)) so it always has a meaningful lower bound. Add a diagnostic field to fitted_params_: _mad_floor_reason = 'computed' | 'floored_on_degeneracy'. Add a DEBUG log when the floor is applied. Files: transforms/nonlinear.py (fit/inverse), tests/training/composite/test_biz_val_composite_transforms.py (logratio edge case).
ideas.test_sketch: Fit logratio on y where base and y are nearly collinear (T_train near-constant). Verify MAD floor is applied, logged, and stored in fitted_params. Predict and ensure that outlier predictions are clipped to the y-bounds (not NaN or inf).

ideas.title: Duplicate row detection and deduplication in discovery fit (MI inflation)
ideas.why: When X has duplicate rows with different y values, the MI(y, X) calculation observes X appearing multiple times with different y values and computes a false-high MI. Discovery then ranks X too high as a base, and the fitted model may be overconfident on the duplicates. This is especially problematic on synthetic / test data with repeated seeds but often undetected in production.
ideas.how: In discovery._fit.fit (or CompositeTargetDiscovery.fit early), add a deduplication check: compute row hashes on X and y, flag rows where X is duplicated but y differs. Log a warning with the count. Optionally (flag-gated): deduplicate by taking the first occurrence before screening MI. Store the dedup indices in self._dedup_mask_ for audit. Files: src/mlframe/training/composite/discovery/__init__.py (fit entry), src/mlframe/training/composite/discovery/_fit.py.
ideas.test_sketch: Create X with 100 rows; duplicate 20 rows but assign different y to the duplicates (simulating label noise). Run discovery and verify that a WARNING is emitted with the duplicate count. Verify that MI ranking is unchanged (off by default) and that enabling dedup lowers the MI of that feature (reduces false-positive signal).

ideas.title: Time-gap detection for recurrent transforms (EWMA / frac_diff) in discovery
ideas.why: EWMA and frac_diff are recurrent-order processes that assume TIME CONTINUITY: missing / NaN rows in base break the time series, and the recurrence carries stale state through the gap. Today there is no time-column detection in discovery; a dataset with date column containing gaps (e.g., weekends in trading data) is treated as if continuous, producing biased alpha estimates and poor forward predictions.
ideas.how: Wire auto_detect_time_column_candidates (already in the module) into CompositeTargetDiscovery.fit: if a time column is detected, compute time-gaps in the sorted-by-time rows and flag base columns that are recurrent (EWMA, frac_diff). If gaps exist, log a WARNING and optionally downrank those transforms (gating via a new config flag check_time_gaps=False). Files: src/mlframe/training/composite/discovery/__init__.py, src/mlframe/training/composite/discovery/_fit.py.
ideas.test_sketch: Create a time series with a gap (e.g., rows at t=0..20, then jump to t=100). Fit discovery and expect a DEBUG log or a lower ranking for EWMA-based candidates if time-gaps are detected. Verify that constant-time (no gaps) data ranks them normally.

ideas.title: Pickle round-trip bit-identity gate for fitted_params_ transforms
ideas.why: CompositeTargetEstimator stores fitted_params_ (alpha, beta, clip bounds) as plain dicts. When pickled and unpickled, floating-point keys / values can drift (especially on numpy arrays serialized as lists). Predictions pre- and post-pickle can diverge by ~1e-7 on the residual scale, failing strict bit-identity tests. Today there is no round-trip validation.
ideas.how: Add a test: fit CompositeTargetEstimator, pickle it, unpickle it, and verify that predict(X_test) is byte-identical on a fixed seed. Use np.testing.assert_array_equal on the raw predictions (not the float-rounded values). If the test fails, investigate numpy dtype downcasting in pickle (e.g., float64 -> float32 on serialization). Files: tests/training/composite/test_composite_pickle_bit_identity.py (new).
ideas.test_sketch: Fit estimator on small synthetic data with a known random seed. Call predict(X) -> y_before. Pickle the estimator, unpickle it, call predict(X) -> y_after. Assert y_before == y_after (exact byte match). Repeat for all major transform families (linear_residual, logratio, ewma, etc.).

ideas.title: Unseen group handling in linear_residual_grouped (fallback strategy)
ideas.why: linear_residual_grouped fits per-group alphas at train time. When a new group appears at predict time (unseen during fit), the current code raises KeyError or silently falls back to the global alpha. There is no explicit strategy to handle unseen groups, and users cannot choose between 'global_alpha', 'raise', or 'nn_impute' (nearest-neighbor group). This is an edge case in production when new categories / segments appear.
ideas.how: Add an unseen_group_strategy parameter to CompositeTargetEstimator.__init__ (default='global_alpha'). In _linear_residual_grouped_inverse, check for unseen groups and apply the strategy: if 'global_alpha', use the global fitted alpha; if 'raise', raise a clear ValueError; if 'nn_impute', find the nearest group (via group-level features stored at fit) and use its alpha. Store which strategy was used in fitted_params_ for auditing. Files: src/mlframe/training/composite/estimator/_estimator.py, src/mlframe/training/composite/transforms/linear.py.
ideas.test_sketch: Fit linear_residual_grouped with 3 groups. At predict time, include a 4th unseen group. With strategy='global_alpha', verify predictions use global alpha (no error). With strategy='raise', verify ValueError is raised. With strategy='nn_impute', verify unseen group is mapped to the nearest known group.

ideas.title: sklearn.clone() robustness for CompositeTargetEstimator with custom transforms
ideas.why: sklearn.clone() calls __init__() with every get_params() entry. When a custom transform (user-registered in _TRANSFORMS_REGISTRY) is no longer available at clone time (e.g., uninstalled dependency, module reload), clone fails silently or produces a broken estimator. Today transform_name is just a string, so clone doesn't catch the registry lookup failure.
ideas.how: Add a _validate_transform_name method called at __init__: check that get_transform(self.transform_name) succeeds and return a callable. If it fails, raise ValueError with a clear message about missing transforms. This ensures clone fails fast with a clear traceback instead of silently producing a broken estimator. Files: src/mlframe/training/composite/estimator/_estimator.py (__init__), tests/training/composite/test_composite_clone_transform_missing.py (new regression test).
ideas.test_sketch: Register a custom transform in _TRANSFORMS_REGISTRY. Create CompositeTargetEstimator(transform_name='custom'). Clone it (should succeed). Then unregister the transform, call clone again, and expect a ValueError with 'transform not found' or similar. Verify the error is raised during clone, not during predict.
